title: A Persona-Based Neural Conversation Model
venue: Proceedings of the 54th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: Can speaker-specific and speaker-addressee representations make neural open-domain responses more consistent with an individual speaker and the relationship between interlocutors?
method: The study augments a four-layer LSTM sequence-to-sequence model with learned speaker embeddings and a dyadic speaker-addressee representation, then compares automatic metrics and crowdsourced consistency judgments with non-personalized baselines.
population_or_data: A 2012 Twitter corpus containing 24,725,711 three-turn sequences from 74,003 speakers, plus dialogue scripts from television series used to test speaker-addressee modeling.
core_finding: Injecting learned speaker or dyadic embeddings improved perplexity, BLEU, and human-judged response consistency over the sequence-to-sequence baseline; the paper reports gains of up to 20% in BLEU and 12% in perplexity in its tested settings.
architecture_implications: Keep a global dialogue model separate from a compact owner-specific representation so personalization can be versioned or replaced without retraining the whole system.
Represent relationship-conditioned behavior separately from global owner behavior because the same speaker may respond differently to different addressees.
Do not treat a latent speaker embedding as factual identity evidence; retain explicit, inspectable claims for facts and consent-sensitive attributes.
limitations: The approach requires many interactions per indexed speaker, evaluates short exchanges rather than longitudinal dialogue, and measures coarse pairwise consistency. Latent embeddings can absorb platform, demographic, and topic confounds and do not expose which evidence caused a response.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "A Persona-Based Neural Conversation Model"
persona speaker addressee embeddings dialogue consistency ACL

title: Personalizing Dialogue Agents: I have a dog, do you have pets too?
venue: Proceedings of the 56th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: Does conditioning open-domain dialogue on explicit profiles for the agent and its interlocutor improve specificity, consistency, and engagement?
method: The authors created PersonaChat by assigning crowdworkers short textual personas and collecting conversations and profile paraphrases, then evaluated retrieval and generative models with profile-memory variants using next-utterance prediction and human judgments.
population_or_data: English crowdsourced personas, paraphrased profiles, and short human-human chats in which participants acted as assigned characters; the released resource contains 1,155 distinct personas used across dataset splits.
core_finding: Models with access to their own profile were judged more persona-consistent, but not more engaging, while training on PersonaChat made models more engaging than training on movie or Twitter dialogue alone. Dialogue also carried enough signal to predict some profile information.
architecture_implications: Store explicit owner claims in an editable profile and retrieve only the claims relevant to the current turn instead of relying solely on an opaque style vector.
Keep self-profile and interlocutor-profile memory distinct, and elicit unknown partner information through conversation rather than guessing it.
Evaluate consistency and engagement separately because improving profile adherence does not guarantee a better conversation.
limitations: PersonaChat uses assigned, synthetic profiles and short single-session conversations, so success measures role adherence rather than fidelity to a real person's changing identity. The paper also found that providing self-profile information did not itself improve engagement.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "Personalizing Dialogue Agents: I have a dog"
PersonaChat explicit profile partner persona next utterance prediction

title: Training Millions of Personalized Dialogue Agents
venue: Proceedings of the 2018 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
research_question: Can persona-conditioned dialogue benefits survive at web scale, and can large noisy persona data improve transfer to a curated persona task?
method: The authors heuristically extracted first-person sentences from Reddit users to form personas, trained response-retrieval models with separate context, persona, and candidate encoders plus a one-hop memory network, and tested direct performance and PersonaChat transfer.
population_or_data: A Reddit dump of 1.7 billion comments was processed into more than 700 million conversation pairs and persona sets covering about 4.6 million users in the rule-based setting; PersonaChat supplied the transfer target.
core_finding: Persona conditioning improved next-response retrieval for bag-of-words, LSTM, and Transformer encoders. Reddit pretraining followed by PersonaChat fine-tuning substantially outperformed training only on PersonaChat in the reported retrieval evaluation.
architecture_implications: Use large conversation histories to propose candidate owner claims, but attach source, time, confidence, and contradiction state because first-person sentences may be transient or context-bound.
A broad base model followed by bounded owner adaptation is more plausible than training a full model from one person's sparse history.
Profile selection should be optimized for downstream utility rather than indiscriminately injecting every historical statement.
limitations: The extraction rules can select temporary states, jokes, or mutually contradictory statements, and the paper observes a strong Reddit-to-PersonaChat domain mismatch. The task retrieves from fixed response candidates rather than generating free-form long-horizon dialogue, and platform-specific user behavior may not generalize.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "Training Millions of Personalized Dialogue Agents"
Reddit millions personas personalized dialogue retrieval EMNLP

title: Personalizing Dialogue Agents via Meta-Learning
venue: Proceedings of the 57th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
research_question: Can a dialogue model adapt to a new persona from only a few example conversations without receiving a hand-authored persona description?
method: The study applies Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning to a Transformer dialogue model, treating conversations grouped by PersonaChat persona as meta-tasks and comparing rapid per-persona adaptation with joint training, profile conditioning, and ordinary fine-tuning.
population_or_data: PersonaChat grouped by persona, with an average of 8.3 unique dialogues per persona; human evaluation sampled 300 generated responses from 10 personas.
core_finding: The meta-learned model adapted from a few dialogues and received higher automatic and human consistency scores than ordinary fine-tuning, even without profile text. Its perplexity and BLEU were worse, reinforcing that common overlap metrics did not track human consistency in this experiment.
architecture_implications: When parameter adaptation is justified, use a small versioned owner adapter initialized for few-shot learning rather than modifying the shared base model.
Gate adapter updates behind enough owner-specific evidence and preserve rollback because a handful of conversations can overrepresent one context or relationship.
Use human or owner consistency evaluation alongside language-model metrics before accepting a personalized checkpoint.
limitations: Evidence comes from PersonaChat rather than naturally evolving users, with few dialogues per persona and a small human-evaluation sample. The experiment does not test corrections, relationship scopes, catastrophic forgetting, or temporal drift after repeated updates.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "Personalizing Dialogue Agents via Meta-Learning"
few-shot persona adaptation dialogue MAML PersonaChat

title: Dialogue Natural Language Inference
venue: Proceedings of the 57th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
research_question: Can persona consistency be formulated as natural language inference and used both to evaluate and improve a dialogue model?
method: The authors derived entailment, neutral, and contradiction pairs from PersonaChat through human-labeled triples and controlled swaps, trained NLI classifiers, and used contradiction confidence to rerank candidate responses before automatic and interactive human evaluation.
population_or_data: Dialogue NLI contains 310,110 training pairs, 16,500 validation pairs, 16,500 test pairs, and a 12,376-pair human-verified gold test set derived from PersonaChat utterances and profile sentences.
core_finding: Dialogue-domain NLI reranking reduced persona contradictions and improved human-rated consistency. In the interactive evaluation, calibrated consistency rose from 0.27 to 0.35 and contradiction fell from 0.25 to 0.16 relative to the underlying profile-memory model.
architecture_implications: Place an independent contradiction checker between generation and final response selection rather than assuming conditioning alone guarantees consistency.
Compare candidate outputs against explicit claims and prior utterances, while retaining neutral or uncertain outcomes instead of forcing every sentence into entailment or contradiction.
Maintain temporal and multi-hop checks beyond sentence-pair NLI for claims whose compatibility depends on time, relationship, or several pieces of evidence.
limitations: The formulation assumes relevant consistency relations fit in sentence pairs and much contradiction data is generated through controlled triple, entity, or numeric swaps. It is tied to PersonaChat-style facts and candidate reranking, so it does not cover multi-hop, temporal, pragmatic, or real-world identity contradictions.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "Dialogue Natural Language Inference"
persona consistency contradiction reranking Dialogue NLI

title: Will I Sound Like Me? Improving Persona Consistency in Dialogues through Pragmatic Self-Consciousness
venue: Proceedings of the 2020 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP)
research_question: Can an inference-time pragmatic listener reduce persona contradictions without additional NLI labels or retraining the generator?
method: The paper wraps existing dialogue agents in a Rational Speech Acts inference procedure with an imaginary listener and learns how to select distractors that expose contradictory candidate responses.
population_or_data: Existing persona dialogue models evaluated on PersonaChat and Dialogue NLI consistency tests.
core_finding: The authors found that strong persona models were often insensitive to the words that made a candidate contradictory. Their pragmatic listener reduced contradictions and improved persona and context consistency on the two evaluated benchmarks.
architecture_implications: Use a model-agnostic inference-time verifier that asks whether an informed listener could reconcile a response with the active owner claims.
Generate contrastive candidates around high-risk facts such as names, locations, dates, relationships, and preferences so small contradictory spans are not hidden by otherwise fluent text.
limitations: The reported evidence is limited to PersonaChat and Dialogue NLI and does not establish long-horizon identity fidelity. Pragmatic reranking adds inference cost and depends on useful distractors; reducing explicit contradiction does not verify that a response is positively grounded in the correct person.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "Will I Sound Like Me?"
persona contradiction pragmatic self-consciousness imaginary listener

title: Like hiking? You probably enjoy nature: Persona-grounded Dialog with Commonsense Expansions
venue: Proceedings of the 2020 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP)
research_question: Can controlled commonsense expansion of sparse persona sentences produce richer responses without sacrificing persona consistency?
method: The model expands persona statements using commonsense knowledge and paraphrase resources, selects among hundreds of expansions with a discrete latent variable, and generates a response grounded in the selected persona content.
population_or_data: PersonaChat dialogues and profiles augmented with candidate commonsense and paraphrase expansions from external resources.
core_finding: Selective commonsense expansion outperformed tested PersonaChat baselines on dialogue quality and diversity while retaining controllable, persona-consistent generation; the study also documents that prior models often miss simple implications of profile statements.
architecture_implications: Represent inferred implications as a separate hypothesis layer, never as confirmed owner facts, and preserve the path back to the asserted claim or external knowledge source.
Select a small context-relevant subset of claims and hypotheses for each turn rather than injecting the entire persona graph.
Require owner confirmation before a commonsense inference can affect durable identity or high-stakes behavior.
limitations: Commonsense associations that are typical for a category may be false for a particular person, and expansion can introduce stereotypes or contradictions. Results are confined to PersonaChat and do not test evolving, relationship-scoped, or owner-corrected profiles.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org persona commonsense expansions "Like hiking"
PersonaChat commonsense expansion controllable consistent response

title: Beyond Goldfish Memory: Long-Term Open-Domain Conversation
venue: Proceedings of the 60th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: How should open-domain dialogue systems retain and use information across multiple conversation sessions?
method: The study collected Multi-Session Chat and compared standard encoder-decoder models with longer contexts, retrieval augmentation, and a read-write system that summarizes new personal information into memory and retrieves it for later generation.
population_or_data: English crowdworker role-play chats extending PersonaChat across multiple sessions: 4,000 training episodes with three sessions, 1,001 with four sessions, and validation/test conversations extending to five sessions, with human-written summaries of personal points.
core_finding: Models trained for short conversations performed poorly on later sessions. Retrieval-augmented and summarization-memory systems improved automatic and human evaluations, with the strongest summarization-memory model referring to partner topics from prior sessions much more often than BlenderBot in the reported human chats.
architecture_implications: Maintain a source-linked episodic conversation record under explicit retention controls alongside compact semantic memory; summaries should point back to their source turns rather than replace them.
Retrieve by current context and relationship, then let the generator attend to a bounded set of prior episodes or summaries.
Evaluate later-session behavior separately because aggregate single-session quality can hide long-term memory failure.
limitations: Participants acted assigned personas, simulated elapsed time, and could be different workers across sessions. The benchmark covers at most five sessions and uses crowd-authored summaries, so it does not establish natural relationship development or safe memory behavior over months or years.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "Multi-Session Chat" persona sessions
"Beyond Goldfish Memory" long-term open-domain conversation

title: Long Time No See! Open-Domain Conversation with Long-Term Persona Memory
venue: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2022
research_question: Can a dialogue system dynamically extract, update, and retrieve both user and bot persona memories without requiring multi-session training data?
method: The authors introduced the LeMon task and DuLeMon dataset, then added a plug-in memory to PLATO with a persona extractor, separate user and bot stores, context-persona retrieval, and a generation/ranking module.
population_or_data: DuLeMon long-term open-domain dialogues with persona grounding annotations for both interlocutors, evaluated with PLATO and PLATO-LTM variants in automatic, human, and self-play settings.
core_finding: Dynamic extraction and retrieval of both parties' persona statements improved long-term consistency and engagement over tested baselines. The paper also reports that task fine-tuning could reduce open-domain coherence in self-play, exposing a specialization trade-off.
architecture_implications: Keep owner memory and interlocutor memory in separate namespaces with separate consent and retention rules.
Implement memory as explicit write, update, retrieve, and generation stages so each mutation is inspectable and reversible.
Monitor general dialogue quality after personalization updates because gains in persona use can coexist with losses in coherence.
limitations: DuLeMon is a constructed and annotated task rather than an organic multi-year relationship corpus, and the evaluated memories are explicit persona sentences. The system does not model evidence provenance, validity intervals, owner corrections, or uncertainty, and its own self-play analysis found a coherence cost from fine-tuning.

site:aclanthology.org "Long Time No See!" persona memory

title: Towards Robust Personalized Dialogue Generation via Order-Insensitive Representation Regularization
venue: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2023
research_question: How sensitive are profile-conditioned dialogue models to arbitrary ordering of persona sentences, and can order-invariant regularization improve robustness?
method: The paper permutes persona sentence order for fine-tuned GPT-2 and BART models, measures response quality and consistency fluctuations, and trains the model-agnostic ORIG regularizer to align representations across permutations.
population_or_data: PersonaChat profiles and dialogues evaluated with GPT-2 and BART personalized generation models.
core_finding: Changing only the order of the same persona sentences caused large performance fluctuations, reported as 29.4% for GPT-2 and 83.2% for BART in the study's analysis. ORIG reduced this order sensitivity and improved consistency.
architecture_implications: Canonicalize unordered claim sets and test prompt or retrieval permutations so arbitrary serialization does not become an accidental priority signal.
Express priority explicitly through confidence, recency, relationship scope, and owner confirmation rather than list position.
Include order-perturbation checks in every persona regression suite.
limitations: Evidence is limited to PersonaChat and two pretrained model families. Sentence permutations test one robustness axis; the paper does not establish robustness to stale, conflicting, adversarial, relationship-specific, or temporally scoped claims.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "order-sensitive" personalized dialogue persona
site:aclanthology.org "Towards Robust Personalized Dialogue Generation via Order-Insensitive Representation Regularization"

title: PeaCoK: Persona Commonsense Knowledge for Consistent and Engaging Narratives
venue: Proceedings of the 61st Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: Can a structured persona commonsense graph supply richer, coherent inferences for dialogue and narrative generation?
method: The authors defined five persona-relation dimensions, gathered candidate persona attributes from existing commonsense graphs and pretrained language models, and used crowdsourcing with model assistance to validate and classify graph edges before downstream evaluation.
population_or_data: PeaCoK, a graph of roughly 100,000 human-validated persona facts spanning characteristics, routines or habits, goals or plans, experiences, and relationships.
core_finding: The resulting graph contained broad, interconnected persona inferences that the paper's analyses and downstream experiments found useful for producing more consistent and engaging narratives.
architecture_implications: A digital brain can use typed edges for characteristics, routines, goals, experiences, and relationships, but every owner edge should also carry source, time, confidence, and confirmation status.
Keep generic commonsense edges in a hypothesis graph separate from person-specific evidence; retrieval may use them to ask a question, not to assert an owner fact.
Graph-derived generation should expose which nodes and edges grounded the response for owner inspection.
limitations: PeaCoK models generic persona-category commonsense rather than a specific person's lived history. Candidate facts partly originate from pretrained models and knowledge graphs, so human validation at dataset level does not make an inference true for an individual; downstream results do not test longitudinal correction or relationship privacy.

title: Character-LLM: A Trainable Agent for Role-Playing
venue: Proceedings of the 2023 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
research_question: Can character-specific supervised fine-tuning on reconstructed experiences produce more faithful role-playing than profile prompting alone?
method: The pipeline collects public character profiles, uses GPT-3.5 to expand profile events into multi-turn scenes with thoughts and emotions, fine-tunes a separate LLaMA-7B model for each character, adds protective experiences that teach knowledge boundaries, and evaluates agents through interviews and model-assisted judging.
population_or_data: Nine historical or fictional characters, each with about 1,400 to 2,200 generated scenes derived primarily from public Wikipedia profiles; the evaluation includes 1,307 single- and multi-turn interview prompts.
core_finding: Character-specific tuning improved memorization and role portrayal relative to prompt-based baselines, and a small set of protective experiences made agents more likely to reject out-of-era questions. The analysis still found that base-model world knowledge could override limited character experience.
architecture_implications: If fine-tuning is used, isolate it in a versioned owner adapter trained only on curated, consented evidence and keep the shared base model immutable.
Train explicit boundary examples for unknown, out-of-time, or out-of-scope questions so fluency does not force fabricated personal knowledge.
Never convert model-generated reconstructions of missing life events into factual memory without owner confirmation and source evidence.
limitations: The study covers nine public historical or fictional characters, relies on GPT-3.5-generated experiences and LLM evaluators, and acknowledges that the scenes cannot represent a whole life. Separate per-character 7B fine-tunes are costly, generated scene details may be fictional, and the setup does not evaluate a consenting living owner.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "Character-LLM" trainable agent role-playing
role-playing character fine-tuning reconstructed experiences protective knowledge

title: MemoryBank: Enhancing Large Language Models with Long-Term Memory
venue: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 38(17)
research_question: Can an external memory with retrieval, summarization, user portraits, and time-dependent updating support persistent personalized LLM conversation?
method: MemoryBank stores conversation memories, retrieves relevant items, generates global event summaries and user portraits, and updates memory strength with a heuristic inspired by the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve; the authors instantiate it in the SiliconFriend companion chatbot.
population_or_data: Qualitative real-world user dialogues plus simulated long-term histories generated by ChatGPT for 15 virtual users across 450 topics and 10 days, with 194 memory-probing questions for quantitative recall analysis.
core_finding: MemoryBank-equipped variants retrieved prior interactions in the simulated probe set and produced examples conditioned on accumulated user portraits; the paper's empathy evidence is a qualitative side-by-side example that the authors interpret as favoring the psychologically tuned SiliconFriend over its ChatGLM baseline.
architecture_implications: Use an external, inspectable memory service with separate event records, summaries, and derived profile hypotheses rather than relying on context windows alone.
Make retention, reinforcement, expiration, editing, and deletion explicit owner-controlled operations; a forgetting score should be a policy parameter, not a claim about human cognition.
Evaluate false recall and harmful over-personalization alongside successful recall because retrieving the wrong memory can be worse than abstaining.
limitations: The broader quantitative evaluation uses 15 simulated users over 10 days and 194 probes, while much of the evidence is qualitative. The forgetting mechanism is a heuristic analogy, not a validated model of personal memory, and the paper does not establish long-term safety, correction behavior, or real-user identity fidelity.

search_queries: site:aaai.org MemoryBank Enhancing Large Language Models with Long-Term Memory
long-term memory personalized LLM companion forgetting curve AAAI

title: Evaluating Very Long-Term Conversational Memory of LLM Agents
venue: Proceedings of the 62nd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: How well do long-context and retrieval-augmented LLM agents understand factual, temporal, and causal information in very long multi-session conversations?
method: The authors generated persona-grounded conversations from temporal event graphs with LLM agents, had humans verify and edit them, and evaluated question answering, event summarization, and multimodal dialogue generation under base, long-context, and retrieval-augmented settings.
population_or_data: LoCoMo contains 10 conversations averaging about 600 turns and 16,000 tokens over as many as 32 sessions, with paired personas, temporal event graphs, and images.
core_finding: Current LLMs struggled with long-range temporal and causal reasoning. Longer contexts and retrieval augmentation improved several tasks but remained substantially below human performance, and dialogue relevance declined as history length grew.
architecture_implications: Index episodic memories by entities, time, event, and causal links rather than relying only on semantic similarity.
Test the digital brain with long-range questions, event summaries, speaker attribution, and temporal ordering, including cases where the correct action is to abstain.
Treat a large context window as one memory access method, not as a replacement for retrieval, provenance, and structured temporal reasoning.
limitations: LoCoMo has only 10 conversations and is primarily LLM-generated, although humans edited it for consistency. It may not capture organic online dialogue, and its web images lack the visual continuity of real personal media; benchmark success therefore does not prove real-world autobiographical memory.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "Evaluating Very Long-Term Conversational Memory" LoCoMo
long-term dialogue temporal causal memory RAG LoCoMo

title: RoleLLM: Benchmarking, Eliciting, and Enhancing Role-Playing Abilities of Large Language Models
venue: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2024
research_question: How can role-playing ability be systematically benchmarked, elicited with prompting, and transferred into open models through role-conditioned instruction tuning?
method: RoleLLM builds profiles for 100 roles, uses Context-Instruct to extract role knowledge and RoleGPT to imitate speaking style, creates RoleBench, and applies role-conditioned instruction tuning to English and Chinese open models.
population_or_data: RoleBench contains 168,093 character-level samples for 100 roles derived from publicly available scripts, with English and Chinese model and evaluation resources.
core_finding: Role-conditioned instruction tuning on RoleBench substantially improved the tested open models' role-playing scores, with RoleLLaMA and RoleGLM approaching the RoleGPT setup that used GPT-4 on the paper's benchmark.
architecture_implications: Separate role knowledge, speaking style, prompt policy, and trainable adapter so each can be updated and evaluated independently.
Use instruction tuning only for stable behavioral patterns; keep mutable facts and temporal memories in retrieval-backed stores.
Maintain dedicated multi-turn tests because high single-turn role scores do not demonstrate long-horizon consistency.
limitations: The framework is designed for single-turn question answering, uses only English and Chinese, and draws from a limited set of public scripts. Much of the instruction data is model-generated, and the evaluation targets fictional or public roles rather than a consenting person's evolving behavior.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org role-playing language model character benchmark persona ACL
site:aclanthology.org "RoleLLM: Benchmarking, Eliciting, and Enhancing"

title: InCharacter: Evaluating Personality Fidelity in Role-Playing Agents through Psychological Interviews
venue: Proceedings of the 62nd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: Can open-ended psychological interviews measure whether role-playing agents reproduce character personality beyond surface knowledge and linguistic style?
method: InCharacter converts items from psychological scales into open-ended interview questions, has role-playing agents answer in character, and uses interviewer LLMs to map responses back to scale ratings for comparison with human-perceived character labels.
population_or_data: Multiple role-playing systems and foundation models portraying 32 characters, assessed on 14 psychological scales against character labels supplied by online sources and human annotators.
core_finding: The interview-based procedure differentiated role-playing agents and found substantial alignment with human-perceived character personalities, with the strongest reported setup reaching 80.7% accuracy on the paper's labels.
architecture_implications: Evaluate a digital brain with open-ended behavioral interviews in addition to factual recall and style matching.
Repeat probes over time and across contexts because a static personality score can conceal drift, situational variation, or prompt sensitivity.
For a real owner, treat psychological-scale outputs as evaluation signals requiring owner interpretation, not as automatic identity facts or diagnoses.
limitations: Scores depend on interviewer LLMs and their biases. Character labels are static even though the paper notes that personalities can change over a storyline, and the study covers fictional or public characters rather than longitudinal owner-grounded behavior.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "InCharacter" personality fidelity role-playing agents
role-playing agent psychological interview personality fidelity ACL

title: TimeChara: Evaluating Point-in-Time Character Hallucination of Role-Playing Large Language Models
venue: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2024
research_question: Can role-playing LLMs respect what a character knew at a specific point in a narrative rather than leaking future or out-of-scope knowledge?
method: The authors built TimeChara through an automated narrative pipeline, tested state-of-the-art LLMs on future-event and participant knowledge, and introduced Narrative-Experts, which decomposes the decision and can retrieve narrative evidence before responding.
population_or_data: 10,895 benchmark instances from four English-language novel series, manually reviewed after automated construction, with role-playing answers evaluated by GPT-4-based judges, AlignScore, and a human comparison.
core_finding: Strong LLMs frequently leaked knowledge inconsistent with the requested character time point. Narrative-Experts reduced these errors, but the paper still found substantial unresolved point-in-time hallucination.
architecture_implications: Give every autobiographical claim and event a validity interval and filter retrieval to information available at the requested time.
Add adversarial tests that ask about future events, superseded beliefs, absent participants, and facts the owner never supplied.
Teach the system to express unawareness when evidence falls outside the active temporal boundary instead of using the base model's world knowledge.
limitations: The benchmark is based on four English-language fictional series and relies heavily on costly LLM judges. Narrative-Experts adds latency and multiple model calls, and fictional timeline fidelity does not by itself validate a real person's uncertain or evolving memories.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org TimeChara point-in-time character hallucination
role-playing LLM temporal knowledge cutoff character consistency

title: LaMP: When Large Language Models Meet Personalization
venue: Proceedings of the 62nd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: How much do user histories help language-model personalization across tasks, and which retrieval strategies best select profile items for prompting or fine-tuning?
method: LaMP defines seven user-conditioned classification and generation tasks and compares non-personalized, zero-shot, and fine-tuned language models with term-matching, semantic, and time-aware retrieval over each user's historical items.
population_or_data: Seven personalization datasets covering three classification and four generation tasks, each organized around multiple historical entries per user profile.
core_finding: Across the benchmark, retrieving selected user-history items improved personalized zero-shot and fine-tuned outputs relative to corresponding non-personalized settings, while retrieval method and task both affected the gains.
architecture_implications: Make retrieval over owner history the default personalization path before considering per-owner fine-tuning because it is inspectable and can be updated or deleted.
Compare semantic, lexical, and time-aware retrieval and optimize for downstream response utility, not similarity alone.
Always include a non-personalized baseline so the system can demonstrate that personal data actually improves the requested behavior.
limitations: LaMP tasks are mostly non-dialogue classification and generation, not sustained interaction, relationship-conditioned behavior, or identity simulation. Dataset metrics measure task output quality rather than owner-perceived authenticity, and historical records may not encode current preferences.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org LaMP When Large Language Models Meet Personalization
personalized language model benchmark retrieval user profile LaMP

title: Crafting Personalized Agents through Retrieval-Augmented Generation on Editable Memory Graphs
venue: Proceedings of the 2024 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
research_question: Can a user-editable graph plus retrieval-augmented generation make personal-device memories more collectable, selectable, and useful to an LLM agent?
method: EMG-RAG represents smartphone memories in an Editable Memory Graph, retrieves graph content for generation, and uses reinforcement learning to optimize data collection, editability, and memory selection for downstream personalized applications.
population_or_data: A real-world smartphone-memory dataset used to evaluate personalized-agent tasks and a transfer of the resulting system into a smartphone assistant; the abstract does not report participant count.
core_finding: The editable-graph RAG system improved the paper's task results by about 10% over the strongest compared approach, and the transferred smartphone assistant produced a reported usability improvement.
architecture_implications: Store durable owner information in an editable graph; for a digital brain, add provenance so nodes and edges can be corrected, superseded, or deleted without retraining a model.
Separate memory truth from retrieval policy: reinforcement learning may choose useful context, but it must not silently rewrite the underlying evidence.
Expose retrieved nodes to the owner and support node- and edge-level correction or deletion so personalization remains inspectable and reversible.
limitations: At abstract inspection depth, participant counts, the exact composition of the real-world dataset, and whether the implementation preserves source provenance or supports source-level deletion are not available. The evaluation is task- and device-specific, and graph extraction or reinforcement-learning rewards can preserve incorrect inferences; long-term identity fidelity and cross-relationship privacy are not established.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "Crafting Personalized Agents through Retrieval-Augmented Generation on Editable Memory Graphs"
personalized agent editable memory graph smartphone RAG EMNLP

title: Comparison of the Intimacy Process between Real and Acting-based Long-term Text Chats
venue: Proceedings of the 2024 Joint International Conference on Computational Linguistics, Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC-COLING 2024)
research_question: Do acted multi-session persona chats reproduce the relationship and intimacy progression observed in real long-term text conversations?
method: The study collected Japanese real long-term chats and matched acting-based Multi-Session Chat conversations, then compared their speech-form choices and dialogue-act distributions as indicators of relationship development.
population_or_data: Japanese real long-term text chats and Japanese acting-based long-term chats in which speakers played defined personas.
core_finding: Acted multi-session chats displayed a mixed and unnatural intimacy pattern: less polite forms suggested excessive closeness while a higher rate of questions suggested a shallower relationship than the real chats.
architecture_implications: Do not use role-play benchmarks as the sole proxy for natural owner relationships; calibrate on consented real interactions or owner judgments.
Model relationship progression and audience-specific language separately from global persona consistency.
Include dialogue-act and register trajectories in long-horizon evaluation, not only fact recall and contradiction rates.
limitations: The study is limited to Japanese chats and selected linguistic and dialogue-act indicators, so it does not establish a universal intimacy model. It compares dataset conditions rather than deployed digital-brain behavior, but directly warns that acted long-term data may have distorted relationship dynamics.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "Comparison of the Intimacy Process between Real and Acting-based Long-term Text Chats"
real versus acting-based Multi-Session Chat intimacy dialogue acts
